voice.name: en-US-Attenborough-Male
voice.prosody:
In 2001, Enron was the seventh-largest company in America. Market cap: $70 billion. Fortune's "Most Innovative Company" for six consecutive years.
One year later, it was worth zero.

voice.name: en-US-ThisAmericanLife-Female
voice.prosody:
This isn't a story about accounting fraud.
It's a story about what happens when smart people become so convinced of their own brilliance that they stop being able to see reality.

voice.name: en-US-Attenborough-Male
voice.prosody: Psychologists call it the overconfidence loop.

voice.name: en-US-Cinematic-Deep
voice.prosody:
And it didn't just destroy Enron — it lives inside every trader, every CEO...
and quite possibly, inside you right now.

voice.name: en-US-Attenborough-Male
voice.prosody: What you're about to learn will change how you read every business headline from this day forward.

voice.name: en-US-ThisAmericanLife-Female
voice.prosody: Every day, somewhere in the world, a CEO is ignoring bad news. A trader is doubling down on a losing position. A fund manager is attributing luck to skill.

voice.name: en-US-Cinematic-Deep
voice.prosody:
The question isn't whether you'll fall into the overconfidence loop.
The question is whether you'll have the courage to admit it before it's too late.

voice.name: en-US-Attenborough-Male
voice.prosody: This is MindMargin. Where psychology meets profit.

voice.name: en-US-ThisAmericanLife-Female
voice.prosody:
If this video changed how you think about business, subscribe.
There are a hundred more stories like this one waiting to be told.
Next week: the cognitive bias that cost a bank $9 billion in 48 hours.
You won't want to miss it.
